event: Rome conquers the region west of the Danube, organizing it into the province of Pannonia.
Emperor Trajan reorganizes the Danubian frontier, with Pannonia forming a key Roman defensive zone.
Pannonia is ceded to the Huns, who build a powerful empire centered on the Carpathian Basin under Attila.
The Avars conquer the Carpathian Basin and dominate the region for over two centuries.
Charlemagne's Frankish forces destroy the Avar Khaganate, opening the basin to new settlers.
The Magyars under Árpád complete their conquest of the Carpathian Basin, founding the Hungarian homeland.
The Hungarians are decisively defeated at the Battle of Lechfeld, ending their raids into Western Europe.
Stephen I is crowned the first King of Hungary, establishing a Christian kingdom.
King Stephen I is canonized as Hungary's patron saint, cementing the realm's Christian identity.
King Andrew II issues the Golden Bull, a charter limiting royal power and granting rights to the nobility.
The Mongols devastate Hungary at the Battle of Mohi, causing massive destruction and population loss.
The death of Andrew III ends the Árpád dynasty, triggering a struggle for the throne.
Charles I of the Angevin dynasty secures the Hungarian throne, beginning a period of consolidation.
John Hunyadi defeats the Ottomans at the Siege of Belgrade, halting their advance into Central Europe.
Matthias Corvinus becomes king, ushering in a Renaissance golden age of culture and strong central rule.
Hungary is crushed by the Ottomans at the Battle of Mohács, killing King Louis II and shattering the kingdom.
The Ottomans capture Buda, dividing Hungary into Ottoman, Habsburg Royal, and Transylvanian parts.
Habsburg-led forces recapture Buda from the Ottomans, beginning the reconquest of Hungary.
The Treaty of Karlowitz ends Ottoman rule over most of Hungary, placing it under Habsburg control.
Francis II Rákóczi launches a major war of independence against Habsburg rule.
The Treaty of Szatmár ends the Rákóczi uprising, confirming Habsburg sovereignty over Hungary.
A revolution erupts demanding civil liberties and independence, led by figures such as Lajos Kossuth.
Hungary declares independence but is crushed by combined Habsburg and Russian armies.
The Austro-Hungarian Compromise creates the Dual Monarchy, granting Hungary broad autonomy.
Buda, Óbuda, and Pest are unified into the single capital city of Budapest.
Hungary celebrates the Millennium of the Magyar conquest with grand national exhibitions and construction.
Austria-Hungary enters World War I, drawing Hungary into a devastating conflict.
The collapse of Austria-Hungary at the end of World War I leads to an independent Hungarian republic.
A short-lived Hungarian Soviet Republic under Béla Kun rises and falls amid foreign intervention.
The Treaty of Trianon strips Hungary of about two-thirds of its territory and population.
Hungary enters World War II on the side of the Axis powers.
Nazi Germany occupies Hungary, leading to the deportation and murder of hundreds of thousands of Hungarian Jews.
Soviet forces drive out German troops, bringing Hungary under Soviet influence.
A Communist-dominated People's Republic is established as a Soviet satellite state.
A national uprising against Soviet domination is crushed by Soviet military intervention.
The New Economic Mechanism introduces market-oriented reforms, creating relatively liberal 'goulash communism'.
Hungary opens its border with Austria and transitions toward multiparty democracy as communism collapses.
Hungary holds its first free multiparty elections in over four decades.
The last Soviet troops withdraw from Hungary, ending decades of occupation.
Hungary joins NATO, anchoring itself in Western security structures.
Hungary becomes a member of the European Union.
A new constitution, the Fundamental Law, is adopted under the Fidesz government.
